answer:
In the *Manusmṛti* (Chapter 7, "The Duties of a King"), *daṇḍa* (the rod)—personified as a divine son of the primordial Lord—is the foundational instrument of royal *dharma*, created expressly "for [the king's] sake... as Dharma, the protector of all beings, formed of the spiritual luster of Brahma."[^1] It ensures cosmic and social order: all beings (even gods, demons, etc.) submit to their duties only through fear of *daṇḍa*, without which "the stronger would roast the weaker like fish on a spit," castes would corrupt, and chaos would ensue.[^2] The king is its rightful wielder (*daṇḍa* "rules all subjects, *daṇḍa* alone protects them"), but only if pure, wise, and deliberative; otherwise, it destroys him and his realm.[^3] Just application requires weighing time, place, ability, and sacred texts; unjust use annihilates everything.[^4] The chapter equates *daṇḍa* with the king himself ("He is the king, the man, *Daṇḍa*"), emphasizing its supremacy over even the Vedas for governance.[^5]

Parallel treatments appear in *Yājñavalkya Smṛti* (Chapters 1–2, on conduct/*ācāra* and legal procedure/*vyavahāra*). Chapter 1's royal duties section (*rājadharmaprakaraṇam*) requires the king to master *daṇḍanīti* (the "science of the rod/punishment") alongside logic, economics, and Vedas; *daṇḍa* is the fourth political expedient (after conciliation, gifts, and division), succeeding when prior measures fail.[^6] It embodies *dharma* ("For *dharma* was created of old by Brahmā in the form of punishment"), wieldable only by the pure and wise, delighting the world if scriptural or enraging it otherwise; just *daṇḍa* yields heaven/fame/victory, while unjust erodes them.[^7] Chapter 2 operationalizes *daṇḍa* via graded fines (*amercements*: highest=1080 *paṇas*, middle/half, lowest/quarter) for debts, theft, assault, etc., scaled by offense, *varṇa*, age, etc.[^8]

*Viṣṇu Smṛti* (Chapter 5) lacks *daṇḍa*'s mythic framing but details its application: graded corporal punishments, fines (*amercements*), branding, banishment, or death (except for Brāhmaṇas, who are branded/banished).[^9] E.g., highest amercement for theft >10 *kumbhas* grain or defiling a Brāhmaṇa; middle for assault drawing blood; lowest for minor insults. Killers of cows/elephants lose hand/foot; false witnesses lose property. The ideal king, via *daṇḍa*, eradicates thieves/adulterers/etc., attaining Indra's world.[^10]

These texts share *daṇḍa* as royal enforcement of *varṇāśrama*dharma: preventive order in *Manusmṛti*, policy/expedient in *Yājñavalkya*, punitive tariffs in *Viṣṇu*.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch.7, [para. p-73f910](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-73f910).
[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch.7, [paras. p-d56424–p-279acb](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-d56424).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch.7, [paras. p-48dedc–p-39e88d](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-48dedc).
[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch.7, [paras. p-ab7b2a–p-6458d4](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-ab7b2a).
[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch.7, [para. p-c970b2](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-c970b2).
[^6]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Ch.1, [paras. p-5552ad, p-20d31d](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-5552ad).
[^7]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Ch.1, [paras. p-ff5556–p-8b92a6](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-ff5556).
[^8]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Ch.2, [whole chapter on vyavahāra](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/), e.g., [para. p-24ff98](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-24ff98).
[^9]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, Ch.5, [paras. p-2c7e24–p-50676b](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/05/translation/#p-2c7e24).
[^10]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, Ch.5, [para. p-cf2b2a](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/05/translation/#p-cf2b2a).
